theorems.title: A large index contains a completely induced subsample
theorems.statementHtml:
If for some sample of size \(i\): \(x_1,\ldots,x_i\) and number \(n\), \(1\leq n\leq i\),

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_i)\geq\Phi(n,i),\]

then there exists a subsample \(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_n}\) of this sample such that

\[\Delta^S(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_n})=2^n.\]

\(\Phi(n,i)\) is defined by the recurrence relation (1).
theorems.proofHtml:
We shall prove the lemma by induction. For \(n=1\), as well as for \(n=i\), the statement of the lemma easily follows from the definition of the index \(\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_i)\) and the fact that, for \(i\geq1\), \(\Phi(1,i)\geq2\) and \(\Phi(i,i)=2^i\). Assume now that the lemma holds for all \(i<r\) and \(n\leq i\) but is false for \(i=r\). In other words, let there exist a sample \(X_r=x_1,\ldots,x_r\) and a number \(n<r\) such that

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_r)\geq\Phi(n,r) \tag{2}\]

and yet the relation \(\Delta^S(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_n})=2^n\) does not hold for any subsample of size \(n\). Then this relation certainly does not hold for each subsample of size \(n\) of the sample \(X_{r-1}=x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1}\). But, by assumption, the lemma is valid for the sample \(X_{r-1}\) and hence

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1})<\Phi(n,r-1). \tag{3}\]

Further, all subsamples induced by the sets in \(S\) in the sample \(X_{r-1}\) may be split into two types. To the first type belongs every subsample \(t\) induced by \(S\) in \(X_{r-1}\) such that only one of the subsamples is induced in the whole sample \(X_r\): either \(t\) or \(t,x_r\). To the second belong those \(t\) for which both \(t\) and \(t,x_r\) are induced in the whole sample. Correspondingly, the set \(S\) is partitioned into two subsets: the subset \(S'\) which induces subsamples of the first type and the subset \(S''\) which induces subsamples of the second type.

Let \(a\) be the number of elements in the set of subsamples of the first type and \(b\) the number of elements in the set of subsamples of the second type. Then the following relations hold:

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1})=a+b, \tag{4}\]

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_r)=a+2b. \tag{5}\]

Taking (3)–(5) into consideration, we have

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_r)<\Phi(n,r-1)+b. \tag{6}\]

Let us now estimate the quantity \(\Delta^{S''}(x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1})=b\). To this end, observe that there exists no subsample \(x_{j_1},\ldots,x_{j_{n-1}}\) of the sample \(x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1}\) for which

\[\Delta^{S''}(x_{j_1},\ldots,x_{j_{n-1}})=2^{n-1}. \tag{7}\]

Equation (7) is impossible since if it were valid, so would the equation

\[\Delta^S(x_{j_1},\ldots,x_{j_{n-1}},x_r)=2^n\]

be valid. The latter is impossible by virtue of the assumption made at the outset of the proof of the lemma. Thus,

\[\Delta^{S''}(x_{j_1},\ldots,x_{j_{n-1}})<2^{n-1}\]

for any subsample of \(X_{r-1}\) of size \(n-1\).

But the lemma holds for the sample \(X_{r-1}\) and hence

\[b=\Delta^{S''}(x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1})<\Phi(n-1,r-1). \tag{8}\]

Substituting (8) into (6), we obtain

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_r)<\Phi(n,r-1)+\Phi(n-1,r-1).\]

Using (1), we have \(\Delta^S(X_r)<\Phi(n,r)\). This inequality contradicts assumption (2). The resultant contradiction thus proves the lemma.

theorems.title: Dichotomy for the growth function
theorems.statementHtml:
The growth function \(m^S(r)\) is either identically equal to \(2^r\) or else is majorized by the power function \(r^n+1\), where \(n\) is a positive constant equaling the value of \(r\) for which the equation

\[m^S(r)=2^r\]

is violated for the first time.
theorems.proofHtml:
As already mentioned, \(m^S(r)\leq2^r\). Suppose \(m^S(r)\) is not identically equal to \(2^r\) and suppose \(n\) is the first value of \(r\) for which \(m^S(r)\ne2^r\). Then, for any sample of size \(r>n\),

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_r)<\Phi(n,r).\]

Otherwise, on the basis of the statement of the lemma, a subsample \(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_n}\) could be found such that

\[\Delta^S(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_n})=2^n. \tag{9}\]

But (9) is impossible, since by assumption \(m^S(n)\ne2^n\). Thus \(m^S(r)\) is either identically equal to \(2^r\) or else is majorized by \(\Phi(n,r)\). In turn, for \(r>0\), \(\Phi(n,r)<r^n+1\).

theorems.title: Symmetrization by two semi-samples
theorems.proofHtml:
By definition,

\[\mathbf P(C)=\int_{X^{(2l)}} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P,\qquad heta(z)= egin{cases}1& ext{for }z\geq0,\0& ext{for }z<0.\end{cases}\]

Taking into account that \(X^{(2l)}\) is the direct product \(X'^{(l)} imes X''^{(l)}\), where \(X'^{(l)}\) is the space of the first semi-samples \(X'_l\) and \(X''^{(l)}\) the space of the second semi-samples \(X''_l\), we have by Fubini’s theorem that

\[\mathbf P(C)=\int_{X'^{(l)}}d\mathbf P'\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P''.\]

Replacing the integration over the whole space \(X'^{(l)}\) by integration over the event \(Q\), we obtain

\[\mathbf P(C)\geq\int_Q d\mathbf P'\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P''. ag{10}\]

By definition, to each fixed semi-sample \(X'_l\) belonging to \(Q\), there exists an event \(A_0\in S\) such that \(|P_{A_0}- u'_{A_0}|>\epsilon\). Thus, to satisfy the condition \( ho^{(l)}_{A_0}\geq\epsilon/2\) or, equivalently, the condition \(| u'_{A_0}- u''_{A_0}|\geq\epsilon/2\), we merely have to require that \(| u''_{A_0}-P_{A_0}|\leq\epsilon/2\).

Coming back to inequality (10), we estimate the inner integral obtaining

\[\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P''\geq\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}_{A_0}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P''\geq\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( rac{\epsilon}{2}-| u''_{A_0}-P_{A_0}| ight)d\mathbf P''.\]

The right-hand side of this last inequality stands for the probability that the difference between the relative frequency and the probability of a fixed event does not exceed \( frac12\epsilon\), i.e.,

\[\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( rac{\epsilon}{2}-| u''_{A_0}-P_{A_0}| ight)d\mathbf P''=1-\mathbf P\!\left(| u''_{A_0}-P_{A_0}|> rac{\epsilon}{3} ight).\]

By Chebyshev’s inequality applied to the binomial distribution,

\[\mathbf P\!\left(| u''_{A_0}-P_{A_0}|> rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)\leq rac{4(1-P_{A_0})P_{A_0}}{\epsilon^2l}< rac{1}{\epsilon^2l}.\]

Therefore, for \(l\geq2/\epsilon^2\),

\[\int_{X''^{(l)}} heta\!\left( rac{\epsilon}{2}-| u''_{A_0}-P_{A_0}| ight)d\mathbf P''> rac12.\]

From this it immediately follows that, for \(l\geq2/\epsilon^2\),

\[\mathbf P(C)\geq frac12\mathbf P(Q).\]

The lemma is proved.
theorems.proofNote: The displayed ε/3 in the probability identity is preserved exactly from the supplied PDF, although the surrounding text and the following Chebyshev bound use ε/2.

theorems.title: Distribution-free uniform deviation bound
theorems.statementHtml:
The probability that the relative frequency of at least one event in class \(S\) differs from its probability in an experiment of size \(l\) by more then \(\epsilon\), for \(l\geq2/\epsilon^2\), satisfies the inequality

\[\mathbf P(\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon)\leq4m^S(2l)e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
By virtue of Lemma 2, it suffices to estimate

\[\mathbf P\!\left( ho^{(l)}\geq rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)=\int_{X^{(2l)}} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P,\]

where \( ho^{(l)}\) is viewed as a function of the sequence

\[X_{2l}=(x_1,\ldots,x_l,x_{l+1},\ldots,x_{2l}).\]

Consider the mapping of the space \(X^{(2l)}\) onto itself resulting from some permutation \(T_i\) of the elements of the sequence \(X_{2l}\). By virtue of the symmetry of the definition of the measure \(\mathbf P\) on \(X^{(2l)}\), the following relation holds for any integrable function \(f(X_{2l})\):

\[\int_{X^{(2l)}}f(X_{2l})d\mathbf P=\int_{X^{(2l)}}f(T_iX_{2l})d\mathbf P.\]

Therefore,

\[\mathbf P\!\left\{ ho^{(l)}\geq rac{\epsilon}{2} ight\}=\int_{X^{(2l)}} rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_i heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P, ag{11}\]

where the summation is over all \((2l)!\) permutations.

Observe further that

\[ heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)= heta\!\left(\sup_{A\in S}| u'_A- u''_A|- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)=\sup_{A\in S} heta\!\left(| u'_A- u''_A|- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight).\]

Clearly, if two sets \(A_1\) and \(A_2\) induce the same subsample in a sample \((x_1,\ldots,x_l,x_{l+1},\ldots,x_{2l})\), then

\[ u'_{A_1}(T_iX_{2l})= u'_{A_2}(T_iX_{2l}),\qquad u''_{A_1}(T_iX_{2l})= u''_{A_2}(T_iX_{2l})\]

and hence, \( ho^{(l)}_{A_1}(T_iX_{2l})= ho^{(l)}_{A_2}(T_iX_{2l})\) for any permutation \(T_i\). This implies that if we choose the subsystem \(S'\subset S\) consisting of all the sets \(A\) that induce essentially different subsamples in the sample \(X_{2l}\), then

\[\sup_{A\in S} heta\!\left( ho_A^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)=\sup_{A\in S'} heta\!\left( ho_A^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)\leq\sum_{A\in S'} heta\!\left( ho_A^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)\]

(the number of elements in \(S'\) is equal to \(\Delta^{S'}(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l})\)). These relations enable us to estimate the integrand in (11):

\[ rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)= rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!}\sup_{A\in S} heta\!\left( ho_A^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)\leq\sum_{A\in S'}\left[ rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho_A^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight) ight].\]

The expression in brackets denotes the quotient of the number of arrangements in a sample (of fixed composition), for which \(| u'_A- u''_A|\geq frac12\epsilon\), and the overall number of permutations. It is easy to see that it is equal to

\[\Gamma=\sum_{k:\,|2k/l-m/l|\geq\epsilon/2} rac{ inom{m}{k} inom{2l-m}{l-k}}{ inom{2l}{l}},\]

where \(m\) is the number of elements in the sample \(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l}\) belonging to \(A\). This expression satisfies the estimate \(\Gamma\leq2e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}\). This estimate can be derived by a simple but long computation and so we omit the proof.

Thus,

\[ rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)\leq\sum_{A\in S'}2e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}=2\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l})e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}\leq2m^S(2l)e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}.\]

Substituting this estimate in the integral (11), we obtain

\[\mathbf P\!\left\{ ho^{(l)}\geq rac{\epsilon}{2} ight\}\leq2m^S(2l)e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}.\]

By virtue of Lemma 2, this yields

\[\mathbf P\{\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon\}\leq4m^S(2l)e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}.\]

To complete the proof, it remains for us to observe that

\[m^S(2l)<(2l)^n+1,\qquad \lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P\{\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon\}\leq4\lim_{l o\infty}[1+(2l)^n]e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}=0.\]

The resultant sufficient condition does not depend on the distribution properties.

theorems.title: Polynomial growth implies uniform convergence in probability
theorems.proofNote: The corollary is printed immediately after Theorem 2. Its proof is the final limit argument in the proof shared with Theorem 2.
theorems.statementHtml: A sufficient condition for the relative frequencies of events in class \(S\) to converge uniformly over \(S\) (in probability) to their corresponding probabilities is that there exist a finite \(n\) such that \(m^S(l)\leq l^n+1\) for all \(l\).
theorems.proofHtml:
To complete the proof, it remains for us to observe that

\[m^S(2l)<(2l)^n+1,\qquad \lim_{l\to\infty}\mathbf P\{\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon\}\leq4\lim_{l\to\infty}[1+(2l)^n]e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}=0.\]

The resultant sufficient condition does not depend on the distribution properties.

theorems.title: Uniform convergence with probability one
theorems.section: 1.5 On uniform convergence with probability one and estimation of the sample size
theorems.proofHtml:
Since

\[\mathbf P(\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon)\leq4m^S(2l)e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}\]

for \(l>l^*=2/\epsilon^2\), the series

\[\sum_{l=1}^{\infty}\mathbf P(\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon)\leq\sum_{l=1}^{l^*}\mathbf P(\pi^{(l)}>\epsilon)+4\sum_{l=l^*+1}^{\infty}[(2l)^n+1]e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}\]

is convergent for any \(\epsilon\). By the lemma, this implies that

\[\mathbf P(\pi^{(l)}\to0)=1.\]

theorems.title: Limit of normalized system entropy
theorems.section: 2.6 Some additional properties of the index
theorems.proofNote: The paper gives no separate derivation and says only that the lemma is proved in the same way as reference [3]. The proof indication precedes the statement.
theorems.statementHtml: The sequence \(H^S(l)/l\) has a limit \(c\), \(0\leq c\leq1\), as \(l\to\infty\).
theorems.proofHtml: The following lemma is proved in the same way as in [3].

theorems.title: Concentration of normalized log index
theorems.section: 2.6 Some additional properties of the index
theorems.proofHtml:
Denote \(\mathbf P(|\xi^{(l)}-c|>\epsilon)\) by \(P(l,\epsilon)\), \(\mathbf P(\xi^{(l)}-c>\epsilon)\) by \(P^+(l,\epsilon)\) and \(\mathbf P(c-\xi^{(l)}>\epsilon)\) by \(P^-(l,\epsilon)\). Accordingly,

\[P(l,\epsilon)=P^+(l,\epsilon)+P^-(l,\epsilon).\]

Lemma 3 implies the existence of an \(l_0\) such that

\[\left|\frac{H^S(l_0)}{l_0}-c\right|<\frac{\epsilon}{4}. \tag{14}\]

We first estimate \(P^+(l,\tfrac12\epsilon)\) with \(l=nl_0\) (\(n\) an integer).

From (13) it follows that

\[\log_2\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{nl_0})\leq\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\log_2\Delta^S(x_{il_0+1},\ldots,x_{(i+1)l_0}).\]

From this we obtain

\[P^+(nl_0,\tfrac12\epsilon)\leq\mathbf P\!\left\{\frac{1}{nl_0}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\log_2\Delta^S(x_{il_0+1},\ldots,x_{(i+1)l_0})-c>\frac{\epsilon}{2}\right\}. \tag{15}\]

Let

\[y=\frac{1}{nl_0}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\log_2\Delta^S(x_{il_0+1},\ldots,x_{(i+1)l_0})\]

and observe that

\[\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\log_2\Delta^S(x_{il_0+1},\ldots,x_{(i+1)l_0})\]

is a sum of independent random variables with expectation \(H^S(l_0)\) and a certain variance \(D\). Hence it follows that

\[\mathbf E y=\frac{H^S(l_0)}{l_0};\qquad \mathbf D y=\frac{D}{nl_0^2}.\]

Using inequality (14) and Chebyshev’s inequality, we obtain

\[\mathbf P\!\left(y-c>\frac{\epsilon}{2}\right)\leq\mathbf P\!\left(y-\frac{H^S(l_0)}{l_0}>\frac{\epsilon}{4}\right)\leq\mathbf P\!\left(|y-\mathbf M y|>\frac{\epsilon}{4}\right)\leq\frac{16D}{n\epsilon^2l_0^2}.\]

This with the help of (15) leads to

\[P^+(nl_0,\tfrac12\epsilon)\leq\frac{16D}{n\epsilon^2l_0^2}\quad\text{and}\quad\lim_{n\to\infty}P^+(nl_0,\tfrac12\epsilon)=0.\]

Let us now prove that

\[\lim_{l\to\infty}P^+(l,\epsilon)=0.\]

For arbitrary \(l>l_0\), let \(n\) be such that \(nl_0<l<(n+1)l_0\). We have

\[\frac{1}{nl_0}\log_2\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{(n+1)l_0})>\frac{1}{l}\log_2\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_l).\]

This leads to

\[\mathbf P\!\left(\frac{n+1}{n}\xi^{(n+1)l_0}>c+\epsilon\right)>P^+(l,\epsilon).\]

But, for sufficiently large \(n\),

\[\mathbf P\!\left(\frac{n+1}{n}\xi^{(n+1)l_0}>c+\epsilon\right)\leq\mathbf P\!\left(\xi^{(n+1)l_0}>c+\frac{\epsilon}{2}\right)=P^+((n+1)l_0,\tfrac12\epsilon).\]

Therefore,

\[\lim_{l\to\infty}P^+(l,\epsilon)=0. \tag{16}\]

We next prove that \(P^-(l,\epsilon)\to0\) as \(l\to\infty\).

From the properties of expectation and the fact that \(\mathbf E\xi^{(l)}=H^S(l)/l\), it follows that

\[\int_0^{H^S(l)/l}\left(\frac{H^S(l)}{l}-\xi\right)dF_\xi=\int_{H^S(l)/l}^{1}\left(\xi-\frac{H^S(l)}{l}\right)dF_\xi. \tag{17}\]

Denoting the right-hand side of (17) by \(R_2\) and the left-hand side by \(R_1\), we estimate them assuming that \(l\) is so large that \(|H^S(l)/l-c|<\epsilon/2\) and obtain first

\[R_1\geq\frac{\epsilon}{2}\int_0^{c-\epsilon}dF_\xi=\frac{\epsilon}{2}P^-(l,\epsilon). \tag{18}\]

Let \(\delta\) be a positive number. Then

\[R_2\leq\left|\int_{H^S(l)/l}^{c+\delta}\left(\xi-\frac{H^S(l)}{l}\right)dF_\xi\right|+\int_{c+\delta}^{1}\left(\xi-\frac{H^S(l)}{l}\right)dF_\xi\leq\left|c+\delta-\frac{H^S(l)}{l}\right|+P^+(l,\delta). \tag{19}\]

Combining the estimates (18) and (19), we have

\[P^-(l,\epsilon)\leq\frac{2}{\epsilon}\left[\left|c+\delta-\frac{H^S(l)}{l}\right|+P^+(l,\delta)\right].\]

This in conjunction with Lemma 3 and (16) implies that

\[\lim_{l\to\infty}P^-(l,\epsilon)\leq\frac{2\delta}{\epsilon}\]

and since \(\delta\) is arbitrary, that

\[\lim_{l\to\infty}P^-(l,\epsilon)=0. \tag{20}\]

Finally, according to (16) and (20),

\[\lim_{l\to\infty}P(l,\epsilon)=0.\]

The lemma is proved.

theorems.title: Entropy criterion for uniform convergence
theorems.statementHtml:
A necessary and sufficient condition for the relative frequencies to converge (in probability) to the probabilities uniformly over the class of events \(S\) is that

\[\lim_{l\to\infty}\frac{H^S(l)}{l}=0. \tag{21}\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Proof of sufficiency. Suppose

\[\lim_{l o\infty} rac{H^S(l)}{l}=0.\]

It will be recalled that, by the lemma, \(2\mathbf P(C)\geq frac12\mathbf P(Q)\). Let us estimate the probability of event \(C\).

As we showed in Subsection 4,

\[\mathbf P(C)\leq rac{1}{(2l)!}\int_{X^{(2l)}}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P.\]

Let \(\delta=\epsilon^2/16\) and split the region of integration into two parts: \(X_1^{(2l)}=\{\log_2\Delta^S(X_{2l})\leq2\delta l\}\) and \(X_2^{(2l)}=X^{(2l)}-X_1^{(2l)}\). Then

\[ egin{aligned}\mathbf P(C)={}&\int_{X_1^{(2l)}} rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P\&+\int_{X_2^{(2l)}} rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P.\end{aligned}\]

Since the integrand does not exceed unity, we have

\[\mathbf P(C)\leq\int_{X_1^{(2l)}} rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)d\mathbf P+P^+(2l,\delta).\]

In Subsection 4 it was shown that

\[ rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta\!\left( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})- rac{\epsilon}{2} ight)\leq2\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l})e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}.\]

Using the fact that \(\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l})\leq2^{2\delta l}\) in the region of integration, we have

\[\mathbf P(C)\leq2\cdot2^{2\delta l}e^{-\epsilon^2l/8}+P^+(2l,\delta)=2(2/e)^{+\epsilon^2l/8}+P^+(2l,\delta).\]

But, by Lemma 4, \(\lim_{l o\infty}P^+(2l,\delta)=0\). Hence it follows that \(\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(C)=0\) and so \(\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(Q)=0\). The sufficiency is proved.

Proof of necessity. \(1^\circ\). Suppose

\[\lim_{l o\infty} rac{H^S(l)}{l}=c>0. ag{23}\]

To prove the necessity, we must show that there exists a positive \(\epsilon\) such that

\[\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(Q)=\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P\!\left\{\sup_{A\in S}| u_A^{(l)}-P_A|>\epsilon ight\} e0.\]

It suffices to estimate the probability of the event

\[C'=\{\sup| u'_A- u''_A|>2\epsilon\}.\]

Indeed, we shall show that from a lower estimate for the probability of event \(C'\) will follow a lower estimate for \(\mathbf P(Q)\). Suppose that \(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l}\) is a given sample and that the event \(Q\) does not occur on both semi-samples, i.e.,

\[\sup_{A\in S}| u'_A-P_A|\leq\epsilon,\qquad \sup_{A\in S}| u''_A-P_A|\leq\epsilon.\]

Then automatically \(\sup_{A\in S}| u'_A- u''_A|\leq2\epsilon\). Thus, taking into account the independence of the semi-samples, we obtain

\[1-\mathbf P(C')\geq(1-\mathbf P(Q))^2,\quad ext{i.e.,}\quad \mathbf P(C')\leq2\mathbf P(Q)-\mathbf P^2(Q).\]

A weakening of this inequality yields \(\mathbf P(Q)\geq frac12\mathbf P(C')\).

\(2^\circ\). Observe now that, by virtue of Lemma 1, one can find a subsample \(x_{i_1},\ldots,x_{i_n}\) of \(X_{2l}\) such that \(S\) induces in it all possible subsamples providing

\[\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_l)\geq\Phi(n,l). ag{24}\]

We assign some \(q\), \(0<q< frac14\), and we estimate the probability of (24) holding for \(n=[ql]\). It is not hard to see that, for \(q< frac14\) and \(n=[ql]\),

\[\Phi(n,l)=\sum_{i=0}^n inom{l}{i}<2 inom{l}{n}<2 rac{l^{[ql]}}{[ql]!}.\]

In what follows, we shall assume that \(l\geq1/q\). Thus \([ql]\geq frac12ql\). Applying Stirling’s formula, we obtain the estimate

\[\Phi(n,l)<2\left( rac{2e}{q} ight)^{ql}.\]

Now for the probability that (24) holds, we obtain the estimate

\[ egin{aligned}\mathbf P\{\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_l)\geq\Phi(n,l)\}&\geq\mathbf P\!\left\{\Delta^S(X_l)>\left( rac{2e}{q} ight)^{ql} ight\}\&=\mathbf P\!\left\{ rac{\log_2\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_l)}{l}>q\log_2 rac{2e}{q}+ rac1l ight\}.\end{aligned}\]

Since \(\lim_{l o\infty}H^S(l)/l=c\), we can choose a sufficiently small positive \(q\) such that

\[q\log_2 rac{2e}{q}<c. ag{25}\]

Assuming further that (25) is satisfied, we can apply Lemma 4 to obtain

\[\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P\{\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_l)>\Phi(n,l)\}=1. ag{26}\]

\(3^\circ\). To complete the proof of the necessity, we just have to estimate

\[\mathbf P(C')=\int_{X^{(2l)}} heta\!\left(\sup_{A\in S}| u'_A- u''_A|-2\epsilon ight)d\mathbf P=\int_{X^{(2l)}} rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})-2\epsilon)d\mathbf P\]

for \(\epsilon>0\).

Choose a \(q\) satisfying (25) and let \(B\) denote the set of those samples for which \(\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l})\geq\Phi([2ql],2l)\). Then

\[\mathbf P(C')\geq\int_B rac{1}{(2l)!}\sum_{i=1}^{(2l)!} heta( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})-2\epsilon)d\mathbf P=\int_BZ\,d\mathbf P.\]

Let us examine the integrand \(Z\) assuming that \(X_{2l}\in B\).

Observe that all permutations \(T_i\) can be classified into groups \(R_i\) corresponding to the same partition into the first and second semi-sample. The value of \( ho^{(l)}(T_iX_{2l})\) does not change within the framework of one group. The number of permutations in all the groups is the same and equal to \((l!)^2\). The number of groups is \( inom{2l}{l}\). Thus,

\[Z= rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{i=1}^{ inom{2l}{l}} heta( ho^{(l)}(R_iX_{2l})-2\epsilon).\]

By Lemma 1, taking into consideration that \(X_{2l}\) satisfies (24) we can pick out a subsample \(y\) in this sample of size \(n\) such that \(S\) induces all possible subsamples in it. The partition \(R_i\) is completely prescribed if the partition \(N_k\) of the subsample \(y\) and the partition \(M_j\) of the subsample \(X_{2l}-y\) are given.

Let \(R_i=N_kM_j\). Let \(r(k)\) be the number of elements in the subsample \(y\) which belong, under the partition \(N_k\), to the first semi-sample and \(s(j)\) the number of elements of subsample \(X_{2l}-y\) which belong, under partition \(M_j\), to the first semi-sample. Clearly, \(r(k)+s(j)=l\) for \(k\) and \(j\) corresponding to the same partition \(R_i\). We have

\[Z= rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_k\sum_j' heta( ho^{(l)}(N_kM_jX_{2l})-2\epsilon),\]

where \(\sum_j'\) is summation over just those \(j\) for which \(s(j)=l-r(k)\), and

\[Z= rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{r=0}^l\left(\sum_k'\sum_j' heta( ho^{(l)}(N_kM_jX_{2l})-2\epsilon) ight),\]

where \(\sum_k'\) is summation over just those \(k\) for which \(r(k)=r\). For each \(N_k\), we can specify a set \(A(k)\in S\) such that \(A(k)\) includes exactly the elements of subsample \(y\) which belong under partition \(N_k\) to the first semi-sample.

Introduce the notation: \(t(k)\) is the number of elements in subsample \(X_{2l}-y\) belonging to \(A(k)\), \(u(k,j)\) is the number of elements in \(X_{2l}-y\) in \(A(k)\) belonging, under partition \(M_j\), to the first semi-sample. Then \( u'_{A(k)}=(r+u)/l\) and \( u''_{A(k)}=(t-u)/l\). Correspondingly,

\[ ho^l_{A(k)}=| u'_{A(k)}- u''_{A(k)}|=l^{-1}|2u+r-t|.\]

We further take into account that \(\sup_{A\in S} ho_A\geq ho_{A(k)}\) and replacing \(\sup_{A\in S} ho_A\) by \( ho_{A(k)}\) we estimate \(Z\) to obtain

\[Z\geq rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{r=0}^l\sum_k'\left(\sum_j' heta(l^{-1}(2u(k,j)+r-t(k))-2\epsilon) ight).\]

Observe that the number of partitions \(N_j\) satisfying the condition \(s(j)=l-r\) for fixed \(r\) is \( inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r}\) and the number of partitions \(N_j\) which in addition correspond to the same \(u\) for fixed \(r\) and \(A(k)\) is

\[ inom{t(k)}{u} inom{2l-[2ql]-t(k)}{l-r-u}.\]

Using these relations, we obtain

\[Z\geq rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{r=0}^l inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r}\sum_k'\sum_u' rac{ inom{t(k)}{u} inom{2l-[2ql]-t(k)}{l-r-u}}{ inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r}},\]

where \(\sum_u'\) is summation over just those \(u\) for which \(l^{-1}|2u+r+t(k)|>2\epsilon\). The expression in the last sum is nothing else than the probability of drawing \(u\) black balls from an urn containing \(2l-[2ql]\) balls of which \(t\) are black, assuming that \(l-r\) balls altogether are drawn without replacement. Moreover (cf. [4]),

\[\mathbf E u= rac{l-r}{2l-[2ql]}t;\qquad \mathbf D u\leq l.\]

Now applying Chebyshev’s inequality, we obtain

\[\mathbf P\!\left(\left| rac{\mathbf M(u)-u}{l} ight|\leq\epsilon ight)\geq1- rac{1}{l\epsilon^2}\]

or

\[\sum_u'' rac{ inom{t}{u} inom{2l-[2ql]-t}{l-r-u}}{ inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r}}\geq1- rac{1}{\epsilon^2l},\]

where the summation is over all \(u\) satisfying

\[\left|u- rac{(l-r)t}{2l-[2ql]} ight|\leq\epsilon l. ag{27}\]

By direct verification it is easy to show that, for \(7\epsilon\leq r/l\leq q+\epsilon\) and \(l>1/\epsilon\), inequality (27) implies that \(|2u+r-t|>2\epsilon l\) for all \(t\), \(0\leq t\leq2l-[2ql]\). Thus, under these conditions,

\[\sum_u' rac{ inom{t}{u} inom{2l-[2ql]-t}{l-r-u}}{ inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r}}\geq1- rac{1}{l\epsilon^2}.\]

Coming back to the estimation of \(Z\), we obtain for \(l>1/\epsilon\)

\[ egin{aligned}Z&\geq rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{7\epsilon\leq r/l\leq q+\epsilon} inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r}\sum_k'\left(1- rac{1}{l\epsilon^2} ight)\&= rac{1-1/l\epsilon^2}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{7\epsilon\leq r/l\leq q+\epsilon} inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r} inom{[2ql]}{r}.\end{aligned}\]

Observe that

\[\lim_{l o\infty} rac{1}{ inom{2l}{l}}\sum_{7\epsilon\leq r/l\leq q+\epsilon} inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r} inom{[2ql]}{r}=1\]

(see, for example, the estimation of \(\Gamma\) in Subsection 4) if

\[0<\epsilon<q/7. ag{28}\]

Finally, assuming that (28) holds, we have for \(l>1/\epsilon\)

\[\mathbf P(C')=\int_BZ\,d\mathbf P\geq\left(1- rac{1}{\epsilon^2l} ight) rac{ inom{2l-[2ql]}{l-r} inom{[2ql]}{r}}{ inom{2l}{l}}\mathbf P(B)\]

and

\[\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(C')\geq\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(B)=\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(\Delta^S(x_1,\ldots,x_{2l})>\Phi([2ql],2l)).\]

We showed in \(2^\circ\) that this last limit has the value 1. Hence it follows that \(\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P(C')=1\). According to \(1^\circ\), this then means that

\[\lim_{l o\infty}\mathbf P\!\left\{\sup_{A\in S}| u_A^{(l)}-P_A|>\epsilon ight\}=1, ag{29}\]

providing

\[\epsilon< rac q7\quad ext{and}\quad q\log_2 rac{2e}{q}<c.\]

Thus, it is possible to choose a positive \(\epsilon\) so that (29) holds. The theorem is proved.
theorems.proofNote: The printed plus sign in the summation condition l^{-1}|2u+r+t(k)|>2ε is preserved from page 279; the surrounding formulas use 2u+r-t.
